skillName: Mental strategies for addition and subtraction
misconceptionSummary: Students round up but forget to adjust back, giving an answer that is too large or too small.

lines.text: Rounded up to 100 but forgot to subtract 3

steps.narration: The student correctly replaced ninety-seven with one hundred but then stopped. Adding one hundred instead of ninety-seven adds three too many — the answer must be reduced by three.

visuals.ruleText: When you round up, you must adjust down by the same amount afterwards

steps.narration: The adjustment is always the gap between the actual number and the rounded number. Ninety-seven is three less than one hundred, so after adding one hundred you subtract three to correct the overshoot.
Three eighty-five plus one hundred is four eighty-five. Subtract the three we overshoot by: four eighty-five minus three is four eighty-two. Always complete the two-step.
Four hundred and eighty-two. Round up, calculate, adjust back — three steps, always.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: adding 100 for 97 and stopping at 485. You over-added by 3, so subtract 3: 485 − 3 = 482. The adjustment step is not optional.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Rounding up borrows from the future — you must pay it back. Add the rounded amount, then subtract the difference between it and the real number.
